case_name: Emily Abney-Acosta v. Robert Santaella, M.D.

summary.brief: Emily Abney-Acosta v. Robert Santaella, M.D.. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Abney-Acosta’s abdomen, and a sponge remained in her abdominal cavity after the surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: During the months following the surgery, Santaella and other physicians aspirated a seroma that developed at the surgical site but did not identify the retained sponge.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Approximately seven months after the surgery and after the wound had healed, Abney-Acosta experienced abdominal pain and Santaella ordered a new CT scan.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The new doctor identified the retained sponge and surgically removed it, thus resolving Abney-Acosta’s symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury found Santaella was not negligent in his treatment of Abney-Acosta.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mayer testified Santaella continued falling below the standard of care in his post-operative care.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Clifford believed Bailey and Magras failed to meet the standard of care and their negligence caused the retained sponge.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Santaella testified surgeons do not participate in counting sponges before or after the surgery, and he 'definitely depends on' the sponge count.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Bailey testified he is responsible for ensuring the sponge count is correct, and stated: 'I should have done a better job.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We affirm the trial court’s judgment.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Abdominal pain and a palpable mass
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient reports feeling a small mass in her abdomen and experiencing abdominal pain rated as an eight on a ten-point scale.

requestables.name: CT scan of the abdomen
requestables.description: CT scan to evaluate the abdominal mass

reveal.decision_impact: Led to decision to surgically remove the mass

requestables.name: Post-operative CT scan
requestables.description: CT scan performed post-operatively to assess complications

reveal.result_summary: Serpiginous metallic density noted, indicating possible retained foreign object
reveal.clinical_significance: Should have prompted further investigation for retained surgical items
reveal.decision_impact: Missed opportunity to identify retained sponge

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with abdominal pain and mass

timeline_phases.name: Post-Operative Care
timeline_phases.description: Care following surgery, including management of complications

key_events.event: CT scan performed showing metallic density

decision_points.clinical_context: During surgery to remove abdominal mass
decision_points.prompt: How should the surgical team ensure no foreign objects are retained?

options.description: Perform a thorough manual inspection and confirm sponge count
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to prevent retained surgical items

options.description: Rely solely on the sponge count provided by the surgical team

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant relied on the sponge count provided by the surgical team
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant stated reliance on team for sponge count

expected_action_court.description: Perform a thorough manual inspection and confirm sponge count
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this is the standard of care

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to perform adequate inspection led to retained sponge
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Manual inspection and confirmation of sponge count
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

decision_points.prompt: What should be done when post-operative imaging shows unexpected findings?

options.description: Review the CT scan and report to identify any retained foreign objects
options.clinical_reasoning: Essential to identify and address complications

options.description: Rely on verbal reports from other physicians without reviewing imaging
options.clinical_reasoning: Misses critical information that could indicate complications

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant relied on verbal reports and did not review the CT scan
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant believed verbal reports were sufficient

expected_action_court.description: Review the CT scan and report to identify any retained foreign objects
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated this is the standard of care

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to review imaging led to delayed identification of retained sponge
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Review of imaging would have identified the issue
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Retained sponge identified and removed, resolving symptoms

key_findings: Jury found Santaella was not negligent
Negligence attributed to surgical team members

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Sponge count and post-operative imaging review
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Review of imaging would have identified retained sponge

factual_timeline.event: Patient consults Dr. Santaella for abdominal mass
CT scan performed showing metallic density

allegations.allegation: Failure to ensure no retained surgical items

court_findings.legal_significance: Jury found no breach of standard of care
